source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.1.2-13.1.3 packet structure (pp. 226-233)

questions.q: What does the TTL (Time To Live) field in an IPv4 header do?
options: Limits the datagram's lifespan/hops: each router decrements it by 1, and at TTL=0 the datagram is discarded
Counts the total bytes of the packet
questions.why: TTL is decremented by each router; when it reaches 0 the packet is dropped, preventing infinite loops.

questions.q: Which protocol numbers identify TCP, UDP and ICMP in the IPv4 Protocol field?
questions.why: The Protocol field uses 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP and 1 for ICMP.

questions.q: What do the DF and MF fragmentation flags mean?
options: DF = Don't Fragment (drop + ICMP error if too big); MF = More Fragments (1 means more fragments follow)
DF = Do Fragment; MF = Must Fragment
DF = Data Flag; MF = Mask Flag
questions.why: DF=1 forbids fragmentation (oversize packets are dropped with an ICMP error); MF=1 means this is not the last fragment.

questions.q: How is the actual IPv4 header length computed from the IHL field?
options: Header length = IHL value * 4 (min IHL 5 = 20 bytes, max 15 = 60 bytes)
Header length = IHL value directly in bytes
IHL is unrelated to header length
questions.why: IHL counts 32-bit words, so bytes = IHL * 4: minimum 5 (20 bytes), maximum 15 (60 bytes).

questions.q: What is the fixed size of the IPv6 basic header?
questions.why: The IPv6 basic header is a fixed 40 bytes with 8 fields; options moved to extension headers.

questions.q: Which IPv6 field is the equivalent of IPv4's TTL?
questions.why: Hop Limit plays the role of IPv4's TTL; Traffic Class corresponds to IPv4's Type Of Service.

questions.q: Why did IPv6 move IPv4's Options into extension headers?
options: IPv4 Options bloat the header (up to 60 bytes) and slow forwarding; IPv6 extension headers form a chain, added only when needed
To make packets larger by default
Because IPv6 has no options at all
To remove the Next header field
questions.why: Extension headers chain via the Next header field and are added only when special processing is needed, keeping the basic header lean.
